source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Paul after this tarried there yet a good while, and then took his leave of the brethren, and sailed thence into Syria, and with him Priscilla and Aquila; having shorn his head in Cenchrea: for he had a vow.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how the Law was breaking up; see how they were bound by conscience.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This, namely, was a Jewish custom, to shear their heads agreeably with a vow.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But then there ought to be also a sacrifice (ch. xxi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26), which was not the case here.—“Having yet tarried:” after the beating of Sosthenes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it was necessary that he should yet tarry, and comfort them concerning these things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He sailed for Syria.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why does he desire again to come to Syria?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was there that “the disciples were ordered to be called Christians” (ch. xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26): there, that he had been “commended to the grace of God” (xiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26): there, that he had effected such things concerning the doctrine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And with him Priscilla”—lo, a woman also—“and Aquila.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But these he left at Ephesus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With good reason, namely, that they should teach.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For having been with him so long time, they were learning many things: and yet he did not at present withdraw them from their custom as Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he came to Ephesus, and left them there: but he himself entered into the synagogue, and reasoned with the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When they desired him to tarry longer time with them, he consented not; but bade them farewell, saying, I must by all means keep this feast that cometh in Jerusalem.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore it was that he was hindered from coming into Asia, being impelled to what was of pressing moment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus observe him here, entreated (by them) to stay, but because he could not comply, being in haste to depart, “he bade them farewell.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However, he did not leave them without more ado, but with promise (to return): “But I will return again unto you, if God will.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he sailed from Ephesus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he had landed at Cæsarea, and gone up, and saluted the Church, he went down to Antioch.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And after he had spent some time there, he departed, and went over all the country of Galatia and Phrygia in order, strengthening all the disciples.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He came again to those places which he had previously visited.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And a certain Jew named Apollos, born at Alexandria, an eloquent man, and mighty in the Scriptures, came to Ephesus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo, even learned men are now urgent, and the disciples henceforth go abroad.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark the spread of the preaching?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This man was instructed in the way of the Lord; and being fervent in the Spirit, he spake and taught diligently the things of the Lord, knowing only the baptism of John.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he began to speak boldly in the synagogue: whom when Aquila and Priscilla had heard, they took him unto them, and expounded unto him the way of God more perfectly.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If this man knew only the baptism of John, how is it that he was “fervent in the Spirit,” for the Spirit was not given in that way?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if those after him needed the baptism of Christ, much more would he need it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then what is to be said?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is not without a meaning that the writer has strung the two incidents together.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems to me that this was one of the hundred and twenty who were baptized with the Apostles: or, if not so, then the same that took place in the case of Cornelius, took place also in the case of this man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But neither does he receive baptism.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That expression, then, “they expounded more perfectly,” seems to me to be this, that he behooved also to be baptized.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because the other twelve knew nothing accurate, not even what related to Jesus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it is likely that he did in fact receive baptism.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if these (disciples) of John, after that baptism again received baptism, was this needful for the disciples also?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And wherefore the need of water?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These are very different from him, men who did not even know whether there were a Holy Ghost.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He was fervent,” then, “in the Spirit, knowing only the baptism of John:” but these “expounded to him more perfectly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he was disposed to pass into Achaia, the brethren wrote, exhorting the disciples to receive him; who, when he was come, helped them much which had believed through grace.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He wished then also to depart into Achaia, and these also encouraged (him to do so), having also given him letters.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Who when he was come, helped them much which had believed through grace: for he mightily convinced the Jews, and that publicly, showing by the Scriptures that Jesus was Christ.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And it came to pass, that, while Apollos was at Corinth, Paul, having passed through the upper coasts”—meaning what we have read as to Cæsarea and the other places—“came to Ephesus, and having found certain disciples (ch. xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1), “he said to them, Have ye received the Holy Ghost since ye believed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they said unto him, We have not so much as heard whether there be any Holy Ghost.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said unto them, Unto what then were ye baptized?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they said, Unto John’s baptism.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then said Paul, John verily baptized with the baptism of repentance, saying unto the people, that they should believe on Him who should come after him, that is, on Christ Jesus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that they did not even believe in Christ is plain from his saying, “that they should believe on Him that was to come after him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he did not say, The baptism of John is nothing, but, It is incomplete.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nor does he add this (in so many words), but he taught them, and many received the Holy Ghost.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “When they heard this, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when Paul had laid his hands upon them, the Holy Ghost came on them; and they spake with tongues, and prophesied.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And all the men were about twelve” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5–7): so that it was likely they had the Spirit, but it did not appear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And all the men were about twelve.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they came to Ephesus, and there he left them” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19): for he did not wish to take them about with him, but left them at Ephesus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they subsequently dwelt at Corinth, and he bears high testimony to them, and writing to the Romans, salutes them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence it seems to me that they afterwards went back to Rome, in the time of Nero, as having an attachment for those parts whence they had been expelled in the time of Claudius.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But he himself went into the synagogue.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems to me that the faithful still assembled there, for they did not immediately withdraw them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they besought him to stay, he consented not” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20, 21), for he was hastening to Cæsarea.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having arrived at Cæsarea,” etc., “passing through the region of Galatia and Phrygia, confirming all the disciples.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Through these regions also he merely passes again, just enough to establish them by his presence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And a certain Jew, Apollos by name,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he was an awakened man, travelling in foreign parts for this very purpose.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Writing of him the Apostle said, “Now concerning Apollos our brother.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Cor. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12.) (β) “Whom when Aquila and Priscilla had heard,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was not for nothing that he left them at Ephesus, but for Apollos’ sake, the Spirit so ordered it, that he might come with greater force to the attack (ἑπιβἥναι) upon Corinth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What may be the reason that to him they did nothing, but Paul they assault?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They knew that he was the leader, and great was the name of the man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he was disposed to pass into Achaia” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 27) i.e. in faith, he did all by faith; “the brethren wrote,” etc. nowhere envy, nowhere an evil eye.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Aquila teaches, or rather this man lets himself be taught.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was minded to depart, and they send letters. (a) “For he mightily convinced the Jews, and that publicly,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now by this, that he “publicly” convinced them, his boldness was shown: by the clearness of his arguing, his power was declared: by his convicting them out of the Scriptures, his skill (of learning).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For neither boldness by itself contributes aught, where there is not power, nor power where there is not boldness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He mightily convinced,” it says. (β) “And it came to pass,” etc. (ch. xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But whence had those, being in Ephesus, the baptism of John?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Probably they had been on a visit at Jerusalem at the time (of John’s preaching), and did not even know Jesus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say to them, Do ye believe in Jesus? but what?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Have ye received the Holy Ghost?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He knew that they had not, but wishes themselves to say it, that having learnt what they lack, they may ask.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “John verily baptized,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From the baptism itself he (John) prophesies: and he leads them (to see) that this is the meaning of John’s baptism. (a) “That they should believe on Him that was to come:” on what kind (of Person)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I indeed baptize you with water, but He that cometh after me, shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when Paul,” it says, “had laid his hands upon them, the Holy Ghost came on them; and they spake with tongues, and prophesied.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6.) (β) The gift is twofold: tongues and prophesyings.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hence is shown an important doctrine, that the baptism of John is incomplete.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, “Baptism” of forgiveness, but, “of repentance.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What (is it) then?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These had not the Spirit: they were not so fervent, not even instructed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why did (Apollos) not receive baptism?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (The case) seems to me to be this: Great was the boldness of the man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He taught diligently the things concerning Jesus,” but he needed more diligent teaching.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus, though not knowing all, by his zeal he attracted the Holy Ghost, in the same manner as Cornelius and his company.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perhaps it is the wish of many, Oh that we had the baptism of John now!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But (if we had), many would still be careless of a life of virtue, and it might be thought that each for this, and not for the kingdom of heaven’s sake, aimed at virtue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There would be many false prophets: for then “they which are approved” would not be very “manifest.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As, “blessed are they that have not seen and yet have believed” (John xx.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 29), so they that (believe) without signs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Except,” saith (Christ), “ye see signs, ye will not believe.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For we lose nothing (by lack of miracles), if we will but take heed to ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We have the sum and substance of the good things: through baptism we received remission of sins, sanctification, participation of the Spirit, adoption, eternal life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What would ye more?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they come to an end (ἀλλὰ καταργεἵται).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou hast “faith, hope, charity,” the abiding things: these seek thou, these are greater than signs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing is equal to charity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For “greater than all,” saith he, “is charity.” (cf.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1 Cor. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But now, love is in jeopardy, for only its name is left behind, while the reality is nowhere (seen), but we are divided each from the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then shall one do to reunite (ourselves)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For to find fault is easy, but how may one make friendship, this is the point to be studied; how we may bring together the scattered members.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For be it so, that we have one Church, or one doctrine—yet this is not the (main) consideration: no, the evil is, that in these we have not fellowship—“living peaceably,” as the Apostle says, “with all men” (Rom. xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 18), on the contrary, we are at variance one with another.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For be it that we are not having fights every day, yet look not thou to this, but (to this), that neither have we charity, genuine and unswerving.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is need of bandages and oil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us bear it in mind, that charity is the cognizance of the disciples of Christ: that without this, all else avails nothing: that it is an easy task if we will.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, say you, we know all this, but how (to go to work) that it may be achieved?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What (to do), that it may be effected? in what way, that we may love one another?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: First, let us put away the things which are subversive of charity, and then we shall establish this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let none be resentful, none be envious, none rejoicing in (others’) misfortunes: these are the things that hinder love; well then, the things that make it are of the other sort.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is not enough to put away the things that hinder; the things that establish must also be forthcoming.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now Sirach tells us the things that are subversive (of friendship), and does not go on to speak of the things which make union.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Reproaching,” he says, “and revealing of a secret, and a treacherous wound.” (cf.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But in speaking of the men of those times, these things might well be named, seeing they were carnal: but in our case, God forbid they should be (even) named.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not from these things do we bring our inducements for you, but from the others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For us, there is nothing good without friendship.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let there be good things without number, but what is the benefit—be it wealth, be it luxury—without friendship?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No possession equal to this, even in matters of this life, just as there is nothing worse than men hating (us).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Charity hides a multitude of sins” (1 Pet. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8): but enmity, even where sins are not, suspects them to be.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not enough not to be an enemy; no, one must also love.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Bethink thee, that Christ has bidden, and this is enough.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Even affliction makes friendships, and draws (men) together.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What then,” say you, “now, when there is no affliction? say, how (are we to act) to become friends?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Have ye not other friends, I ask?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In what way are ye their friends, how do ye continue such?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For a beginning, let none have any enemy: this (in itself) is not a small matter: let none envy; it is not possible to accuse the man who envies not. (b) How then shall we be warmly affected?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What makes love of persons?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Beauty of person.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us also make our souls beautiful, and we shall be amiable one to another: for it is necessary, of course, not only to love, but also to be loved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us first achieve this point, that we may be loved, and the other will be easy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How to act that we may be loved?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us become beautiful, and let us do this, that we may always have lovers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let none make it his study to get money, to get slaves, to get houses, (so much) as to be loved, as to have a good name.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Better is a name than much wealth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the one remains, the other perishes: and the one it is possible to acquire, the other impossible.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he that has got an evil character, will with difficulty lay it aside: but by means of his (good) name the poor man may quickly be rich.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let there be a man having ten thousand talents, and another a hundred friends; the latter is more rich in resources than the former.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not merely do this, but let us work it as a kind of trade.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And how can we?” say you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “A sweet mouth multiplieth its friends, and a gracious tongue.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us get a well-spoken mouth, and pure manners.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not possible for a man to be such, and not to be known.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (a) We have one world that we all inhabit, with the same fruits we all are fed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But these are small matters: by the same Sacraments we partake of the same spiritual food.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These surely are justifications of loving! (c) Mark how many (inducements and pleas) for friendship they that are without have excogitated; community of art or trade, neighborhood, relationships: but mightier than all these are the impulses and ties which are among us: this Table is calculated more (than all else) to shame us into friendliness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But many of us who come thereto do not even know one another.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The reason, it may be said, is that there are so many of them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By no means; it is only our own sluggish indifference.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Once) there were three thousand (ch. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 41)—there were five thousand (iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4)—and yet they had all one soul: but now each knows not his brother, and is not ashamed to lay the blame on the number, because it is so great!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet he that has many friends is invincible against all men: stronger he than any tyrant.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not such the safety the tyrant has with his body-guards, as this man has with his friends.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover, this man is more glorious than he: for the tyrant is guarded by his own slaves, but this man by his peers: the tyrant, by men unwilling and afraid of him; this man by willing men and without fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And here too is a wonderful thing to be seen—many in one, and one in many. (a) Just as in an harp, the sounds are diverse, not the harmony, and they all together give out one harmony and symphony, (c) I could wish to bring you into such a city, were it possible, wherein (all) should be one soul: then shouldest thou see surpassing all harmony of harp and flute, the more harmonious symphony. (b) But the musician is the Might of Love: it is this that strikes out the sweet melody, (d) singing, (withal) a strain in which no note is out of tune.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This strain rejoices both Angels, and God the Lord of Angels; this strain rouses (to hear it) the whole audience that is in heaven; this even lulls (evil) passions—it does not even suffer them to be raised, but deep is the stillness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as in a theatre, when the band of musicians plays, all listen with a hush, and there is no noise there; so among friends, while Love strikes the chords, all the passions are still and laid to sleep, like wild beasts charmed and unnerved: just as, where hate is, there is all the contrary to this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us say nothing just now about enmity; let us speak of friendship.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Though thou let fall some casual hasty word, there is none to catch thee up, but all forgive thee; though thou do (some hasty thing), none puts upon it the worse construction, but all allowance is made: every one prompt to stretch out the hand to him that is falling, every one wishing him to stand.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A wall it is indeed impregnable, this friendship; a wall, which not the devil himself, much less men, can overpower.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not possible for that man to fall into danger who has gotten many friends.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Where love is) no room is there to get matter of anger, but only for pleasantness of feeling: no room is there to get matter of envying; none, to get occasion of resentment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark him, how in all things both spiritual and temporal, he accomplishes all with ease.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then, I pray you, can be equal to this man?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Like a city walled on every side is this man, the other as a city unwalled.—Great wisdom, to be able to be a creator of friendship!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Take away friendship, and thou hast taken away all, thou hast confounded all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if the likeness of friendship have so great power, what must the reality itself be?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us, I beseech you, make to ourselves friends, and let each make this his art.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, lo! you will say, I do study this, but the other does not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All the greater the reward to thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, say you, but the matter is more difficult.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I testify and declare to you, that if but ten of you would knit yourselves together, and make this your work, as the Apostles made the preaching theirs, and the Prophets theirs the teaching, so we the making of friends, great would be the reward.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us make for ourselves royal portraits.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if this be the common badge of disciples, we do a greater work than if we should put ourselves into the power to raise the dead.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The diadem and the purple mark the Emperor, and where these are not, though his apparel be all gold, the Emperor is not yet manifest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So now thou art making known thy lineage.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Make men friends to thyself, and (friends) to others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is none who being loved will wish to hate thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us learn the colors, with what ingredients they are mixed, with what (tints) this portrait is composed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us be affable: let us not wait for our neighbors to move.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say not, if I see any person hanging back (for me to make the first advances), I become worse than he: but rather when thou seest this, forestall him, and extinguish his bad feeling.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou one diseased, and addest to his malady?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This, most of all, let us make sure of—“in honor to prefer one another, to account others better than one’s self” (Rom. xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10), deem not this to be a lessening of thyself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou prefer (another) in honor, thou hast honored thyself more, attracting to thyself a still higher extinction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On all occasions let us yield the precedence to others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us bear nothing in mind of the evil done to us, but if any good has been done (let us remember only that).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing so makes a man a friend, as a gracious tongue, a mouth speaking good things, a soul free from self-elation, a contempt of vain-glory, a despising of honor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we secure these things, we shall be able to become invincible to the snares of the Devil, and having with strictness accomplished the pursuit of virtue, to attain unto the good things promised to them that love Him, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, dominion, honor, now and ever, world without end.
